perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information shall be held in stri ct confidence by Receiving Party and shall not be used by Receiving Party except for (i) the pur poses described in this Agreement, unless disclosure or further use is authorized or consented to in writing by Disclosing Party which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld or dela yed and/or (ii) the case where disclosure of the same is required under applicable law or by a gove rnmental order, rule or regulation or by the regulations of any relevant stock exchange or other governmental authority (provided that the Receiving party shall give written notice of such re quired disclosure to the other party prior to the disclosure).
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information shall be held in stri ct confidence by Receiving Party and shall not be used by Receiving Party except for (i) the pur poses described in this Agreement, unless disclosure or further use is authorized or consented to in writing by Disclosing Party which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld or dela yed and/or (ii) the case where disclosure of the same is required under applicable law or by a gove rnmental order, rule or regulation or by the regulations of any relevant stock exchange or other governmental authority.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to provide written notice prior to disclosing proprietary information when required by law, governmental order, etc., the contract increases the risk of inadvertent or unauthorized disclosure that could harm the Disclosing Party. This conflicts with Delaware's emphasis on protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the DUTSA by eliminating a procedural safeguard (written notice) that allows the Disclosing Party to take steps to protect its information before legally mandated disclosure occurs.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The omission of the written notice requirement directly undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially leading to misappropriation as defined in Delaware Code Title 6 Â§ 2001 (2). The original text ensured the Disclosing Party had an opportunity to protect its trade secret before mandatory disclosure. By removing this safeguard, the changed text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure without allowing the Disclosing Party to take protective measures, thus creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon a request by the Disclosing Pa rty at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Inform ation of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Inform ation and any and all copies or extracts thereof or upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that all materials containing Proprietary Information (incl uding all copies thereof) have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Immediately upon a request by the Disclosing Pa rty at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Inform ation of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Inform ation and any and all copies or extracts thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the option for the Receiving Party to certify in writing that all materials containing Proprietary Information have been destroyed, the contract eliminates a verification method that the Disclosing Party could use to confirm compliance, potentially creating legal risks related to trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the destruction certification option the agreement undermines the reasonable efforts required under DUTSA to protect trade secrets. The omission eliminates a key means for the disclosing party to verify that its proprietary information has been fully removed from the receiving party's possession and control.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes a verification method (certification of destruction) for proprietary information, potentially undermining reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the omission eliminates a key means for verifying the removal of proprietary information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as acquisition or disclosure of a trade secret by improper means. By removing the certification option, the contract weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to ensure the Receiving Party does not misappropriate trade secrets, because this verification is part of the reasonable efforts to keep Trade Secrets secure. Thus, the altered text contradicts the need to take reasonable steps to protect trade secrets, as required to prevent misappropriation.
